network.overall_strategy: Combine multiple prebiotic pathways—Strecker, phosphoro-Strecker, thioester chain elongation, Fischer-Tropsch, formose and cyanosulfidic chemistries—across hydrothermal and surface environments converging on key intermediates (3-hydroxypropanal, α-ketobutyrate) to build threonine non-enzymatically.

reactions.name: Strecker synthesis of threonine aminonitrile
reactions.mechanism: Nucleophilic addition of HCN and NH3 to the aldehyde forming an aminonitrile
reactions.reasoning: Oro & Kimball, 1961 demonstrated Strecker synthesis of α-aminonitriles under prebiotic conditions (Source 6940)

reactions.name: Hydrolysis of threonine aminonitrile to threonine
reactions.mechanism: Acid-catalyzed nitrile hydrolysis to yield the amino acid
reactions.reasoning: Miyakawa & Ferris, 2002 showed aminonitrile hydrolysis on mineral surfaces to α-amino acids

reactions.name: Phosphoro-Strecker formation of phosphoro-aminonitrile
reactions.reasoning: Xu & Patel, 2015 described phosphoro-Strecker synthesis of N-phosphoroaminonitriles at neutral pH (Sources 21192, 21193)

reactions.name: Hydrolysis of phosphoro-aminonitrile to threonine
reactions.mechanism: Hydrolytic conversion of N-phosphoroaminonitrile to amino acid
reactions.reasoning: Xu & Patel, 2015 demonstrated subsequent hydrolysis of phosphoroaminonitriles to amino acids (Sources 21192, 21193)

reactions.name: Thioester-mediated chain elongation to α-ketobutyrate
reactions.mechanism: Thioester coupling of glyoxylate and pyruvate followed by rearrangement
reactions.reasoning: Smith & Morowitz, 1996 proposed thioester-driven chain elongation of α-keto acids under hydrothermal conditions (Source 995)

reactions.name: Reductive amination of α-ketobutyrate to threonine
reactions.mechanism: Nonenzymatic reductive amination of the α-keto acid with ammonia
reactions.reasoning: Weber & Miller, 1981 showed reductive amination of α-keto acids on Fe0 surfaces yields amino acids (Source 1026)

reactions.name: Aldol condensation to 3-hydroxypropanal
reactions.mechanism: Clay-catalyzed base-mediated aldol addition of acetaldehyde and glycolaldehyde
reactions.reasoning: Kitadai et al., 2017 demonstrated montmorillonite-catalyzed aldol condensations under prebiotic conditions

reactions.name: Formose reaction producing glycolaldehyde
reactions.reasoning: Butlerow, 1861 first described the formose reaction yielding glycolaldehyde (Source 15634)

reactions.name: Fischer-Tropsch-type synthesis of acetaldehyde
reactions.mechanism: FTT catalytic hydrogenation of CO2 to aldehydes
reactions.reasoning: McCollom & Seewald, 2007 showed magnetite-catalyzed FTT synthesis of small aldehydes in vents (Sources 19309, 19315)

reactions.name: Glycolonitrile formation from formaldehyde and HCN
reactions.mechanism: Photocatalytic nucleophilic addition of HCN to formaldehyde
reactions.reasoning: Patel et al., 2015 described glycolonitrile formation under cyanosulfidic conditions (Source 9376)

reactions.name: Photolysis of glycolonitrile to 3-hydroxypropanal
reactions.mechanism: UV-driven cleavage of glycolonitrile yielding aldehyde and regenerating HCN
reactions.reasoning: Patel et al., 2015 demonstrated cyanosulfidic photolysis pathways forming sugars and aldehydes (Source 9376)

reactions.name: Fischer-Tropsch-type synthesis of α-ketobutyrate
reactions.mechanism: FTT chain growth yielding α-keto acids
reactions.reasoning: McCollom & Seewald, 2007 reported FTT synthesis of α-keto acids under vent-like conditions (Source 19315)

pathways.name: Hydrothermal FTT to surface Strecker
pathways.description: FTT production of acetaldehyde in vents, surface aldol to 3-hydroxypropanal, Strecker and hydrolysis to threonine

pathways.name: Direct surface Strecker
pathways.description: Surface Strecker reaction of 3-hydroxypropanal with HCN and NH3 followed by hydrolysis

pathways.name: Hydrothermal thioester chain elongation
pathways.description: Thioester-mediated elongation from glyoxylate and pyruvate to α-ketobutyrate then reductive amination to threonine

pathways.name: Formose-aldol surface route
pathways.description: Formose reaction yields glycolaldehyde, aldol with acetaldehyde to 3-hydroxypropanal, then Strecker and hydrolysis

pathways.name: Cyanosulfidic glycolonitrile route
pathways.description: Photocatalytic formation and photolysis of glycolonitrile yield 3-hydroxypropanal, then Strecker/hydrolysis

pathways.name: Surface phosphoro-Strecker
pathways.description: DAP-mediated Strecker on 3-hydroxypropanal yields phosphoroaminonitrile then hydrolysis to threonine

pathways.name: Hydrothermal FTT to phosphoro-Strecker
pathways.description: FTT acetaldehyde, surface aldol to 3-hydroxypropanal then phosphoro-Strecker route

pathways.name: FTT-aldol hydrothermal amination
pathways.description: FTT α-ketobutyrate produced in vents, surface aldol yields 3-hydroxypropanal then hydrothermal reductive amination

pathways.name: Direct hydrothermal amination
pathways.description: FTT production of α-ketobutyrate in vents followed by direct reductive amination to threonine

pathways.name: Mixed FTT and thioester elongation
pathways.description: Combined FTT generation of α-ketobutyrate and thioester‐mediated chain elongation then amination

pathway_evaluations.feedback: P1 integrates FTT and Strecker chemistry but omits the generation of glycolaldehyde required for the aldol step.
suggestions: Include rxn_008 (formose reaction) to generate glycolaldehyde prior to rxn_007.
Specify availability of glycolaldehyde or treat it as a starting molecule.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Direct Strecker route is sound but lacks the prebiotic production of 3-hydroxypropanal (mol_006).
suggestions: Add an upstream reaction (rxn_007 or rxn_011) to supply mol_006.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Thioester chain elongation and reductive amination are plausible but pyruvate (mol_005) is not produced in the network.
suggestions: Incorporate a reaction to generate pyruvate or mark it as a starting feedstock.
Detail catalytic conditions for thioester formation.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Formose–aldol–Strecker sequence is chemically valid but assumes acetaldehyde availability without FTT support.
suggestions: Precede rxn_007 with rxn_009 to generate acetaldehyde.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Strong cyanosulfidic route with clear generation of mol_006 and seamless Strecker conversion to threonine.
suggestions: Optionally include formaldehyde production (e.g., formose) to close the loop.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Phosphoro-Strecker steps are plausible but rely on an undefined source of 3-hydroxypropanal (mol_006).
suggestions: Add an upstream aldol or cyanosulfidic step to generate mol_006.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Combines FTT and phosphoro-Strecker chemistries but lacks glycolaldehyde to form mol_006.
suggestions: Clarify integration between hydrothermal and surface stages.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Path uses reductive amination on the wrong intermediate; does not produce α-ketobutyrate required for rxn_006.
suggestions: Realign the amination step to follow α-ketobutyrate formation or replace rxn_006 with a Strecker step.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Direct hydrothermal FTT to α-ketobutyrate followed by reductive amination is coherent, but direct C4 FTT yields may be low.
suggestions: Detail mineral catalyst (e.g., magnetite) and conditions for C4 FTT.
Consider a two-stage chain elongation via thioesters.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Mixed FTT and thioester elongation lacks production of pyruvate and misaligns FTT output with chain-elongation input.
suggestions: Generate pyruvate upstream or use FTT to produce pyruvate first.
Clarify the role of each hydrothermal step.

evaluation.network_feedback: The network robustly integrates known prebiotic chemistries (Strecker, phosphoro-Strecker, cyanosulfidic, thioester, FTT, formose) across hydrothermal and surface settings. Key strengths include multiple convergent routes and plausible environmental transitions. Improvements are needed in completing source reactions for intermediates such as glycolaldehyde, formaldehyde, and pyruvate, and specifying mineral catalysts and conditions.
